Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                              Traveller [TRAV-uh-ler, TRAV-ler]. –noun. [Origin circa
                              1300 CE, from the Middle English travaillour, the treader
                              of a path, emphasizing labor or toil, as in “to make a dif-
                              ficult journey”]. 1. One who travels, or who has traveled,
                              or who will travel, as to distant places. 2. An adventurer.
                              3. The game of science-fiction adventure in the far future.


                              Deraabelar [der-AAB-el-ur, de-RAAB-e-lur] DERAQBE-
                              LAR. –noun. [Origin circa - 2200 Imperial, loan word from
                              the Anglic, but with connotations of distance because of
                              its similarity to the Vilani rabelar RABELAR, far away].
                              1. One who goes far away, or has gone far away and re-
                              turned. 2. A rogue. 3. A popular Terran role-playing game
                              transplanted to the worlds of the First Imperium.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Traveller Is A Role-Playing Game
                                  Traveller is a role-playing game. Simply put, Players assume Roles as Charac-
                              ters in the universe of the far future. These Characters journey to the many differ-
                              ent worlds of the galaxy, encountering unique challenges which may bring rewards
                              or disaster, but which always bring adventure.

                    Players use Traveller to live through stories, governed by rules, in which fictional characters, controlled by one or more
                players, act within a fictional reality governed by a game master.
                    The Game Master is the moderator who presents (and often creates) the game situation, accepts input and action deci-
                sions by the players, and implements the rules results. The Game Master is, in various games, also called the DM, Dungeon
                Master, Judge, GM, Game Master, or in Traveller: the Referee.
                    The Players are the real world participants in the game. Their in-game personalities in Traveller are their Characters.
                    Traveller describes a consistent universe of the far future, complete with an extensive history and a vast array of hu-
                man and non-human inhabitants. Traveller also provides a comprehensive set of game rules which address many (but could
                never address all) of the activities characters can expect to undertake.

                     Traveller is a co-operative game: there are no winners          How To Play
                or losers. Characters are friends: members of a group who                 Players sit around a table equipped with paper, pen, var-
                travel together in pursuit of their various goals; they work         ious game reference books, and dice. The Referee presents
                together rather than at cross-purposes. They may be the              the Players with situations and asks for their responses.
                crew of a starship, soldiers-in-arms, or even random people               Referee: “You have just arrived in this remote star sys-
                brought together by chance or fate. They travel together,            tem. Now you stand on the observation deck of your Imperial
                make group decisions, supplementing, and complementing               explorer. The reason for the anomalous signal you detected
                each other as they explore and interact with the universe.           is now apparent: a derelict ship drifting above a barren world.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                        The Galaxy




                                     Charted Space


                      The Galaxy (also called Our Galaxy, the Milky Way Galaxy, Galaxias1, or Dakhaseri2) is the barred spiral galaxy that
                is home to Humaniti and thousands of other alien intelligent species. In size, some 30 kiloparsecs in diameter and 300 par-
                secs thick at its center, it contains an estimated 400 billion star systems. The Galaxy is almost as old as the universe itself: its
                oldest stars date to less than a billion years after the beginning of time. The majority of its stars, however, are middle-aged:
                between 6 and 10 billion years old. Most of its stars have planetary systems with habitable zones capable of producing and
                maintaining life.
                                                                                      1. Galaxias. Literally, Milky [Greek]. There is an ancient Terran
                      In some of the Galaxy’s systems, life has come and gone: fable that the band of bright light visible across the heavens is
                their worlds bear the marks of long-dead civilizations. In other breast milk spilt by the queen of the gods
                systems, the time for life has not yet arrived: someday, life may 2. Dakhaseri. Literally, Audience of Stars [Vilani]. An ancient Vilani
                yet arise in their primordial seas. But in many, their worlds have story tells of meritorious souls allowed to watch the events of the
                intelligent life with cultures and societies almost too strange to world; their discussions (and futile attempts to intervene) are the
                                                                                      basis of many Vilani myths.
                understand.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                  A Brief History of the Universe
                                 The universe teems with life: everywhere, worlds coalesce from gas and star-
                              dust; everywhere, life begins; everywhere, life evolves toward intelligence. On
                              some of those worlds, intelligence reaches for the stars, and on some of those
                              worlds, intelligence succeeds.
                     The history of the universe is the history of the exploits of many different intelligent species and their interactions (a eu-
                phemism for expansion, aggression, and conflict) with other intelligent species.
                     This history is divided broadly into milieux (plural; the singular is milieu) or eras dominated by two controlling principles:
                a few intelligent species, and a few important facts. Each milieu is focused on some specific step in the progress (or temporary
                decline) of interstellar civilization. Finally, each milieu had a long-lasting and profound influence on future generations.

                LONG, LONG AGO                                                         a mutation, incredibly intelligent, incredibly talented, and in-

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                          About 9300 BCE, an exploratory expedition to a nearby
                                                                                    system discovered intelligent life, a race of aquatic mantas.
                                                                                    Soon thereafter a colonial expedition to another discovered
                                                                                    archeological evidence of a human civilization which had
                                                                                    died out several thousand years before.
                                                                                          About 9200 BCE, routine experiments in energy stor-
                                                                                    age led to the discovery of the Jump Drive:2 the key to FTL
                                                                                    Faster-Than-Light travel. The Vilani were empowered and
                                                                                    transformed. They alone, of everyone they visited, had the
                                                                                    secret of fast interstellar travel. They kept their technology a
                                                                                    monopoly and used it to dramatically expand their territory,
                                                                                    which ultimately became a star-spanning empire: the Ziru
                                                                                    Sirka.3 Its 7,000-year reign can be divided into three periods:
                                                                                          The Early Imperium (9200 BCE to 5400 BCE). Heady
                                                                                    with their newfound technology, the Vilani explored hundreds
                                                                                    of star systems; they encountered dozens of sophont spe-
                                                                                    cies. With their monopoly on FTL, the Vilani dominated every
                                                                                    culture they met, even those with higher technology. For the
                                                                                    early empire, this was a time of rapid expansion and easy
                work (or that still perform incomprehensible functions).            domination.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                The Foundations of the Traveller Universe
                                  Traveller is a comprehensive science-fiction game system spanning a major
                              portion of the galaxy and reaching far into the future and far into the past. Fun-
                              damental to the system are answers to many questions about life, society, and
                              civilization in the universe. Everything is part of a cohesive structure that gradu-
                              ally unveils itself… to the participants and to observers (whether they are readers,
                              viewers, or players).
                    Traveller describes a vast future universe in which mankind has already reached the stars and conquered thousands of
                worlds, but still faces the never-ending struggle to conquer more worlds and wrest more secrets from the universe.
                    Traveller uses a foundation of hard science, supplemented by the soft sciences to add character and flavor, and driven
                by characters, to explore the worlds and cultures of the future universe, all in search of adventure.

                BASED ON A ROLE-PLAYING GAME                                             The Jump Drive and JumpSpace concepts make star

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                         The cosmopolitan universe is nevertheless human-
                                                                                      dominated, primarily for a sense of familiarity for the players.

                                                                                      Everything Is Driven By Economics
                                                                                             Economics is not strictly the study of finance; it is the
                                                                                      study of making choices between and among limited possi-
                                                                                      bilities. Regardless of the pronouncements of political, mor-
                                                                                      al, or cultural leaders, action in this universe takes place be-
                                                                                      cause it will produce some economic advantage. Economic
                                                                                      advantage generally means rewards in a monetary sense,
                                                                                      but it can also mean rewards in political or social power. Be-
                                                                                      hind all lies some economic motive.

                                                                                           Players can understand what happens in Traveller be-
                                                                                      cause it is driven by the same elements that drive all human
                                                                                      (or sophont) endeavor: economics.

                                                                                      There Is No “Prime Directive”
                                                                                           Most interstellar governments encourage development,
                races, and individuals. People (and the term is used to re-           especially economic development. Governments rarely im-
                fer to “beings”) come in many different forms, all of whom            pose “Prime Directives” (that undeveloped cultures and so-
                constantly interact as a matter of course. Unless local cir-          cieties be allowed to develop without interference until they
                cumstances require a homogeneous local population, travel-            can enter the community of interstellar civilizations). Instead,
                lers will continually encounter local populations which reflect       economic forces drive exploration and trade.
                diversity in terms of age, gender, and race. Even apparently
                homogeneous groups will demonstrate (upon closer exami-                  There are no artificial rules constraining player action.
                nation) more diversity than expected.                                 Nevertheless, their actions have (potential) consequences.

                    Traveller accepts diversity and allows (even requires)            Duty, Honor, and Loyalty
                a wide variety of beings to interact for their mutual benefit.             Interstellar society naturally values people (human or
                Such a universe is richer than a purely human environment.            not) on whom it can depend: those who are loyal and who
                                                                                      faithfully do their duty are the ones to whom society awards

                strife between various groups, but the universe itself allows         ciety who faithfully and with innovation follow the orders of
                any individuals with talent to rise to the top of their field.        their superiors. At the same time, superiors have learned to
                      Conflict. Differences breed conflict. In many cases, that       express their orders in the most general of terms: to give
                conflict can be resolved with words and negotiation. On the           greater freedom of action.
                other hand, violence and the threat of violence are integral
                parts of the universe of the Traveller universe.                           People with responsibilities are expected to act respon-
                                                                                      sibly. If they do not, they won’t hold their positions for long.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Traveller Uses Dice
                                 Traveller uses dice to make outcomes uncertain. There’s always a chance that
                              some effort will fail. There are situations where a random answer is more suitable
                              than a choice by the player.
                     Dice are randomizers (or random number generators) in Traveller.
                     Traveller uses Six Sided Dice. Only D6 dice are used in Traveller. Sometimes, in order to remain true to the D6 concept,
                the system contorts D6 die rolls to achieve special results (for example, even distributions from 1 to 10 or 1 to 9).

                BASIC TERMS                                                       against a standard. Check <Kilometers> instructs the user
                   The following basic terms apply to dice and die rolls:         to compare 2D versus distance in kilometers; if the result
                                                                                  is less than (or equal to) the distance, the roll is successful.
                About Dice                                                             Check is assumed to be 2D unless otherwise stated.
                     Traveller consistently addresses dice.                       Easy Check assumes 1D; Hard Check assumes 3D. Other
                     Dice. The randomizers used in Traveller are ordinary         dice are specified (5D Check Kilometers; 10D Check Hours).
                six-sided cubic dice marked with sides marked 1 to 6 (with
                pips or numbers). Dice is plural; the singular is Die.            About Modifiers
                     Die Roll. The result of rolling one or more dice.                  Target Number (or just Target). The number the player
                     D. Abbreviation for dice. In Traveller, this is always a     is trying to roll. Some uses of dice call for a specific number
                six-sided die. Other game systems may use different dice.         to be rolled (Target=5). Others call for a number or less to
                     D6. Another abbreviation for six-sided dice. Technically     be rolled (Target=5 or less). Still others call for a number or
                the 6 refers to outcomes: one per side. A die with 10 (equally    greater to be rolled (Target=5 or more). In each case, the
                probable) outcomes (and 10 sides) is a D10. A die with 3          focus is on a Target Number.
                outcomes (but strangely has 4 sides) is a D3. This terminol-            Mod. Modifier. A change to the Target Number. A Modi-
                ogy reiterates that the dice used are six-sided and have six      fier increases or decreases the Target Number (in contrast to
                equally probable results.                                         a DM which alters the actual Die Roll).

                About Die Rolls                                                   Mods Are Primarily Used With Target Numbers
                     Traveller consistently addresses die rolls.                       DM. Die Mod. Die Modifier. Dice Modifier. A change to
                     Roll or Throw. An instruction to roll dice. For example,     the Die Roll. A DM increases or decreases the result of the
                Roll 2D instructs the player to roll two dice.                    dice before it is compared to the Target Number (in contrast
                     Check. An instruction to roll dice and compare the result    to a Mod which changes the Target Number itself).




                DICE: BE PREPARED
                  The referee and each player should have a pool of readily available six-sided dice in a variety of colors:
                  Ten Six-Sided Dice are available for standard die rolls. Actually, any of the dice shown here can be used for standard rolls.
                Ten dice is sufficient for the most extreme of rolls: 10D Double Hasty Beyond Impossible.
                  Two Contrasting Color Dice are used for Flux rolls. The lighter color is always positive; the darker is always negative.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Money
                                The value of objects and labor is measured in Money. The varied systems of
                              money in use across the universe allow individuals to buy and sell, to accumulate
                              wealth, to settle debts, and to acquire objects.
                     Money spans a range of values: the money needed for one person to survive is often just a rounding error for a giant cor-
                poration. The money required to finance and maintain a starship might give its captain a lifetime of luxury on his homeworld,
                but at the cost of foregoing a lifetime of adventure.

                THE THREE LEVELS OF MONEY                                                                BARTER
                     Money is accounted for in three distinct                                                 Barter is a direct transaction without the
                levels, each with its own purpose and level                                              use of money. Such Barter transactions di-
                of efficiency.                                                                           rectly trade one type of goods for another;
                                                                                                         each participant acquires the specific goods
                Credits (Cr)                                                                             they want in a quantity that makes each side
                     The basic personal form of money is                                                 equal.

                vices are expressed in Credits. One Credit                                               The Frontier Trader’s Ramp

                tenth-hour) of unskilled labor.                                                          he can try to barter with the natives using the

                Lone Star: he is paid several credits for an                                             or other goods he feels will be attractive to
                hour of work, who then uses his pay to buy                                               the natives.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                       Humanity
                                 Most player characters are Human: players already understand the many roles
                              Humans can play. A player who fully understands how to play a Human character
                              can ultimately progress to playing non-Humans.
                    The standard player character in Traveller is Human for convenience in role-playing. The Traveller5 rules set is built
                around Human standards and is easily understood by Human players.

                HUMANITY IS A BENCHMARK
                      The character is a central focus of Traveller5 as a role-playing game. Because players best understand the abilities
                and activities of Humans, Humanity is an easily understood benchmark of performance. The rules for vision make sense to a
                player because he already understands how vision works and what outcomes are reasonable. The rules for strength reflect
                reality. The rules for accomplishing tasks are understood because the player already understands likely outcomes.
                      Beyond Humanity. A player can expand his role-playing to Traveller5’s non-Human options: clones, chimeras, synthet-
                ics, robots, artificials, and sophonts are all understandable when compared to the known benchmark: Humanity.

                The History Of Humanity And Humaniti                              The Major Human Races

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
1
                                 eHex


                                  E           Expanded
                                              Hex Code


                                  The Traveller Expanded Hex Code (eHex) substitutes single letters for numbers above 9 and allows the creation of a
                             string of character with values from 0 through 33. Hexadecimal numbers use A, B, C, D, E, F for 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15 respec-
                             tively to create a base-16 number system (as used in some computer systems). The letters I (eye) and O (oh) are omitted to
                             avoid confusion with 1 (one) and 0 (zero).
                                  The Expanded Hex Code allows representation of values through 33 using single letters, facilitating the number-string
                             profiles used in Traveller. For example, the Universal Personality Profile (UPP) represents the six personal characteristics in
                             a string (in the UPP Human format SDEIES). Using single number digits, the values for each profile digit can range from 0 to
                             9. Using Hex Code, those digit values can range from 0 to 15. Using the Expanded Hex Code, alphabetic letter values can
                             range from 0 to 33. They may also (in some usages) be assigned arbitrary values or non-numeric meanings.

                             NUMBER TO EHEX                                                                                          EHEX TO NUMBER

                                                                                                                                          Tons
                                   1 ton= 13.5 cubic meters
                   The ton is a standard unit of volume commonly used throughout
                Charted Space. It has applications in starship design, payload measure-
                ment, and size designations.
                                                                                                        Standard
                                                                                                 Units Of Volume                      T
                THE BASIS OF THE TON                       STANDARD CARGO PODS
                      A ton is the volume of 1,000 kilo-
                grams of liquid hydrogen and equal
                to approximately 13.5 cubic meters.
                      Maritime Tons. In classic wa-
                ter-based shipbuilding, a ton is the
                volume of 1,000 kilograms of water.
                A ship with a displacement of 1 ton
                afloat in water displaces one ton of
                water.
                      Starship Ton. A 100-ton ship
                submerged in liquid hydrogen dis-
                places 1350 cubic meters (100 tons)
                of liquid hydrogen.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                         Distances and Range Bands
                                  Distance is an open-ended concept of separation between objects: it governs
                              the effectiveness of the senses and of sensors, of weapons, attacks, and com-
                              munications; it determines travel times. Range Bands distill these many possible
                              distances into a set of easily used typical distances.

                      There is an infinite number of distances and a corresponding infinity of POSSIBLE exact calculations based on distance.
                It is possible (but unwieldy) to express exact distances for every situation. It is 11.4 kilometers to the starport; it is 47.3 meters
                to that red warning sign; that flyer is 87,342 meters above us.
                      Traveller distills the open-ended concept of distance into a series of common range bands, each associated with a
                typical distance and identified by one or more benchmarks. The sacrifice in detail is more than made up in ease of use. The
                starport is Distant; that warning sign is at Short range; that flyer is at Upper altitude.

                THE RANGE BANDS                                                       The Lettered Space Range Bands
                      Range Bands define typical distances, standardized for              One of the Space S= Range Bands is lettered.
                convenience, and quantified for comprehension. Further de-                Boarding Range= B occupies the gap between S= 1
                tail rarely adds enough realism to outweigh the complexity.           and S= 0 (and includes S= 0). It is the range between ships
                      Relative Distances: Range Bands show relative dis-              at which Boarding can be attempted.
                tances between an observer (or a Zero Point such as a
                world surface). Each Range Band has a descriptive name                Sub-Bands
                and a distance value. For example, Medium Range is typi-                    Any Range Band can be divided into several decimal
                cally 150 meters: somewhere between Short Range (about                Sub-Bands when the distinction is important. Most often,
                50 meters) and Long Range (about 500 meters).                         some altitudes need further differentiation.
                      Available Distances. Each Range Band covers about                     Mid-Band. The easiest sub band is half-way. Sub Band
                half-way from the previous Range Band to about half-way               X.5 allows identification of a Range midway between two es-
                to the next range band. For example, Medium Range Band                tablished Range Bands.
                extends from about 100 meters to about 300 meters.                          Layered Sub-Bands. Greater differentiation within a
                                                                                      Sub Band is possible by specifying four even decimal divi-
                Two Distinct Range Types                                              sions 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8).
                     For ease of use, two different Range types are avail-                  For example, to reflect various layers within the atmo-
                able: World Surface Ranges and Space Ranges.                          sphere of a Gas Giant, Range 6 is subdivided Bands 6.2,
                     World Surface Ranges (R=) are useful with individu-              6.4, 6.6, and 6.8 (which reflect different pressures).
                als, with small arms, and with events on or near worlds.                    Decimal Sub-Bands. It is possible to identify all nine
                     Space Ranges (S=) are useful with spacecraft, with               decimal Sub-Bands (0.1 through 0.9) for extreme precision.
                space weapons, and with events within a star system.                  It is almost never necessary to divide a Range Band into
                                                                                      decimal hundredths (6.02, or 6.13).
                World and Space Ranges are convertible                                      The purpose of Sub-Bands is to differentiate distances

                 The Size of a Star System
                    The Travel Charts 8 and 9 provide insight into the size and accessibility of a star system (and into the performance capa-
                 bilities of ships).
                    Terra is Orbit O= 3; Uranus is Orbit O= 8. Assuming the two planets are on the same side of the star system, Table 8a (How
                 Far?) indicates they are at Range S= 13.
                    A ship under constant 1G acceleration, Table 9a (Start and Stop Arrival Time) shows that it can reach S= 13 and stop at a
                 world in about 8 days. Under constant 9G acceleration, it can arrive in about 2 days.
                    A hostile ship at about the orbit of Uranus can launch a 1G Kinetic Kill Missile toward a target near Terra S=13. Table 8b
                 (How Fast?) indicates that it will achieve Speed=13; Table 9b (Constant Acceleration Impact Time) shows that the time ti
                 impact is about (and impact) in 152 hours. At 9G, it would impact in 51 hours.
                    Terra is Orbit O= 3; Jupiter is Orbit O= 5. They are about 4.2 AU apart at their closest; or 6.2 AU apart at their greatest
                 separation. They are, in either case, in the same Range Band S= 12. A ship capable of 2G (Table 8b) can make the journey
                 in about 3 hours.

                 Effects (Applies to All Tables)
                    R= Range (or Depth from Zero).
                    P= Pressure in Bars (Terra Surface Pressure= 1). P Effect is Blast-P: P=4 produces Blast-4= 4D hits.
                    H= Turbulence in Dx Hits (Calm atmosphere= 0). Effect is Blast-H: H=5 produces Blast-5= 5D hits.
                    T= Temperature. Positive T Effect is Hot-T (T= 6 is Hot-6); Negative T Effect is Cold-T (T= - 6 is Cold-6).
                    Values above 99 are exponents (^2= 10^2= 100; ^3= 10^3= 1000).

                            18    16     4,900      3,687 3,932 4,178 4,424 4,670 4,916 5,407 5,898 6,390 6,881 7,373                               16
                            19    17     9,800      7,373 7,864 8,355 8,847 9,338 9,830 10,797 11,764 12,731 13,698 14,665                          17
                            When necessary, star-world distances can be varied deliberately, or with ux. In the rare instance where two adjacent
                            orbits coincide (as in O=9 plus 0.5 and O=10 minus 0.5), the result is a special case.
                            The Decimal Orbital Distances table shows values in AU to a tenth of an orbit; if necessary, values can be calculated to a
                            hundredth of an orbit or greater (but observe the MOARN principle: Map Only As Really Necessary).

                  10                Fame, Risk,

                                      Danger, and Threats

                                                                                  FAME
                                                                                        Fame measures notoriety, or reputation. Fame is
                                                                                  the degree of recognition or respect which society (or
                 10a FAME, DANGER, AND THREATS                                    subdivisions of society such as the military) holds for an
                                                                                  individual. Fame is not a guarantee of recognition; it is
                     Distance   F= Descriptor           Alternate Descriptor
                                 0
                                                                                  the likelihood that a search of documents or databanks
                                   Unknown
                                                                                  will return information about the individual, or that a dis-
                                 1 Parent, Person       1 person.                 cussion will include reference to the person.
                                 2 Close Family         10 people. Ship crew.           Express Fame as Fame-<level>. A world-famous
                                 3 Extended Family 100 people.                    entertainer has Fame-10. A Fame-10 entertainer prob-
                                                                                  ably has name recognition anywhere on his world.
                                 4 Neighborhood         1,000 people.
                                                                                        Alternate Descriptors reflect Organizations or So-
                                 5 Town                 10,000 people.            cieties (as opposed to standard distance-based Fame).
                                 6 City                 100,000 people.                 Infamy: Fame may be negative; note magnitude
                      10 km      7 Large City           One million people.       and ignore sign.
                     100 km      8 Regional             Corporation.
                                                                                  DANGER AND THREATS
                    1000 km      9 Continental                                         Danger is the potential for Harm; Threat is the
                                10 World                Large Corporation.        source or agent of a Danger. Harm includes death, de-
                                11 World  Complex                                 struction, injury, illness, reduction in ability, or significant
                         S= 4   12 World System         Powerful Corporation.     change which rational beings would believe to be harm-
                                                                                  ful or undesirable.
                        S= 11   13 Inner System                                        Expressing Danger and Threat. Express Threat-
                                14 System                                         <level> and Danger-<level> using the Fame scale.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Benchmarks
                                 Understanding the unfamiliar is easier when players have standards against
                              which it can be compared.
                   A Benchmark is a standard by which objects, concepts, or values can be compared or evaluated. Benchmarks are ex-
                amples which make other examples easier to understand. Traveller Benchmarks provide insights into three distinct concepts.
                   Benchmarks for Value and Cost provide insights into the value of money and how it can be earned and used.
                   Benchmarks for Size provide an understanding and useful measure of relative size.
                   Benchmarks for Range help understand distance and its effect on the senses, sensors, weapons, and travel times.

                Benchmarks For Value And Cost                                       expect to earn a salary in the range of Cr750 per month
                     Benchmarks support basic financial information on              (which, according to The Costs of Living table, is slightly
                which economic activities are based.                                more than an average character needs to live).
                     Salaries and Wages. Characters can expect to receive                For example, a Spectacular Entertainer with Fame-10
                payment for their labor based on specific standards. By             can expect to earn Cr240,000 per year.
                knowing the benchmarks, the player can understand if an                  Salaries are appropriate for Scholars, Entertainers,
                offer of employment will pay wages which are too low or too         Scouts, Naval, and Military characters.
                high, and they can then react accordingly.                               Wages. The Wages table shows typical hourly wage
                     The Cost of Living. The typical costs of housing, meals,       structures for various skill types. Some characters may de-
                and other details give players insights into basic costs which      cide to work for wages rather than salary if that is more ad-

                     Upon completion, the vessel is delivered to the buyer,
                with the bank paying off the purchase price to the shipyard.
                Because the bank now holds title to the ship, the price must
                be paid off in a series of monthly payments to it.
                     Standard financing terms are:

                      Monthly Payments
                      equal to 1/240 of initial loan amount
                      for 480 months
                      with an effective interest rate of about 4%.

                UNDERSTANDING SIZE                                                                                                                      1
                    Size indicates the approximate size or dimensions of an object.
                The chart shows the basic benchmark sizes. For example, the referee
                may say,
                    “You see a Person-Size something in the distance.”
                    “You see a Size-5 object on the starport tarmac.”
                                                                                                                          Size               S
                    “Sensors detect a Size-5 object separating from that ship.”

                                                             Size is broadly descriptive with some overlap. Person-Size may indicate anything
                                R             T         larger than a suitcase and smaller than a truck. Suitcase-Size may indicate anything
                                                        larger than a book and smaller than a person.
                                                             Sizes can be decimal. An object slightly smaller than Size-6 is Size 5.9; a slightly
                                                        larger object is Size 6.1.
                                                             Carrying And Using. A Size-N item can typically carry and use any object less
                              Needle         Word       than its own Size. For example, a Size 5 Sophont can carry and use a Size 4 Object. A
                                                        Size-6 Truck can carry several Size-5 objects.
                              Reading    Talking
                                                             Size And World Range (Or Distance) Are Related. A person with ordinary vision
                               0.5 m      1.5 m         can usually see an object of Size-N or larger at Range-N or less.

                 LAND GRANT VALUE
                     An unimproved Land Grant generates income based on the Trade Classifications of the world and equal to Cr10,000 per

                                                                             - 44 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                               Benchmarks
                                                                                                                                                      1
                                                                                                                      Value               V
                THE WORTH OF THINGS
                     An object has a value to an individual based on a variety of factors. Value is defined in money, but may be evaluated in
                other than money terms (a treasured picture of a parent may be worth a great deal to a son, and nothing at all to a stranger).
                     Cost Refers To Production. A manufacturer who creates an object encounters a cost in money based on the elements
                put into the object, the labor required, and a suitable allowance for overhead.
                     Price Refers To Sales. The amount for which an object is sold to the consumer is the price.
                     Value Is Relative. Somewhere between an object’s cost to make and its sale price is its value.
                     Buying And Selling Is A Win-Win Situation. A Seller wants to sell for more than his cost. The Buyer wants to pay less
                than (or equal to) an object’s value. When a buyer and seller make a transaction, it is possible for both to win.


                  THE VALUE RULE
                                                                                          Major                   Large       Small
                      Typical         Snack        Meal        Clothes    Device          Part         ATV       Vehicle      Craft     Starship
                       Salary                      1 hr                    1 mo            1 yr

                  Value                    0           1           2             3             4           5          6           7           8
                      Credits              <1             10       100         1,000       10,000     100,000       MCr1       MCr10     MCr100
                     The table shows Values. A manufacturer or producer can usually make these goods (in quantity) paying less than Value.
                  A buyer can usually buy these goods individually for Value or slightly more.

                VALUES FOR OBJECTS
                      Values are orders of magnitude and help in estimating              Volume Production. An enterprise can manufacture a
                object costs and prices through simple logic.                       quantity of objects at a cost less than their final value.
                      Base Value is a very rough indication of the worth of an           Very Efficient Production Cost              = Value / 10
                object. A good meal is worth about Cr10. A cook could pay                Mass Production Cost                        = Value / 5
                a grocery cost of Cr5, prepare a meal, and price it at Cr10.             Small Manufacturer (100 items) Cost         = Value / 3
                      Cost Modifications. The cost (manufacturing cost, pro-             Individual Assembler (20 Items) Cost        = Value / 2
                duction cost, wholesale cost) is some fraction of the Base               Price Modifications. The price for an object may be
                Value taking into account volume production, production dif-        influenced by Supply and Demand, or by QREBS, or both.
                ficulty, resource availability, and available technology.

                                                                               - 45 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Characters Are The Central Focus Of Traveller
                                 Characters are the central focus of Traveller; they are the alter-egos of the
                              players, and all activity is centered on them.
                     Each character is a person: usually Human, possibly non-Human, conceivably synthetic, or robotic, or geneered, or even
                a disembodied intelligence.
                     Whatever its form, the character is the Traveller personality; the player is the person engaged in playing the game. Once
                a player has generated a character, he or she proceeds to live and adventure until killed in action, or until too old and decrepit
                to keep up. The first step for any player is to create a character.




                 CHARACTER POSSIBILITIES*

                 Human
                  Imperial (an undifferentiated sort of commonly encountered Human).
                  Vilani (the branch of Humaniti that first reached the stars).
                  Solomani (the original Humans of Terra).
                  Cassildan (unusually tall Humans evolved on a low gravity world).
                  Geonee (unusually short Humans evolved on a high gravity world).

                 Non-Humans
                  Aslan (vaguely feline sophonts from regions spinward of the Imperium)
                  Vargr (canid sophonts from regions coreward of the Imperium).
                  Hivers (strange starfish-like sophonts from regions trailing the Imperium).
                  K’kree (centaur-like militant herbivores from regions trailing the Imperium).
                  Bwaps (roughly reptilian sophonts known for their administrative abilities).
                  Ursa (uplifted bears created by genetic modification projects on Terra).
                  Virushi (gentle giant many-legged sophonts with a penchant for science).

                 Others
                   Humaniform Robots (constructed in imitation of Human form).
                   Strangeform Robots (intelligent machines originally built for industry)
                   Synthetic Persons (grown in genetic factories for industry and service).
                   AI (residing in a ship computer, and occasionally a disposable robot body).
                 *This list is only a sampling.

                                     Every Player Plays A Role:
                                          Each Player literally becomes a Character– human,
                                     non-human, robot, any gender, any career – and through
                                     that Character experiences the many adventures the uni-
                                     verse of the far future has to offer.
                                          Player: the individual playing Traveller.
                                          Character: the role the Player assumes.
                                                                             - 46 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                 Characteristics Establish A Foundation
                                         Every Character has six basic digital values that describe his or her physical,
                                     mental, and social abilities. These Characteristics establish a foundation for the
                                     individual’s interaction with the universe. They are gathered into a six-character
                                     string (the UPP Universal Personality Profile) for easy reference.

                     Characteristics are basic information about a Character: how strong he is, how smart, how agile, even how important.
                Each Character has six basic Characteristics: three physical, two mental, and one social. In addition, experience may reveal
                other obscure Characteristics.
                     Characteristics Are Assets. The central Task Resolution mechanic for Traveller is based on Assets: abilities and re-
                sources that the individual Character brings to the situation. Characteristics are important as assets which may enable the
                individual to accomplish his goals.
                     Characteristics Are Descriptive. At even the simplest level, Characteristics provide an understanding of who an indi-
                vidual is. High Strength says a Character is Strong; low Dexterity says a Character is Clumsy; moderate Intelligence says a
                Character’s thinking and reasoning ability is Ordinary.

                CHARACTERISTICS FOR SOPHONTS                                                                                           Non-Human Characteristics. It is also possible for
                    Traveller describes sixteen different Characteristics.                                                       Characters to be non-human: sophonts (intelligent aliens)
                Most characters have six (plus two special). The specific six                                                    from their own worlds and cultures and civilizations. They
                are determined by the individual’s species.                                                                      may be similar to Humans and have Characteristics in com-
                    Human Characteristics: The most common type of                                                               mon with Humans, or they may be radically different with
                Character is Human.                                                                                              Characteristics which Humans do not have. The 16 common

                PHYSICAL VS MENTAL VS SOCIAL VS OBSCURE
                    Characteristics are generally divided into categories:
                Physical, Mental, Social, or Obscure. Each character has
                three Physical characteristics, two Mental Characteristics,
                and one Social Characteristic. The Obscure characteristics
                may appear as play progresses.

                Physical Characteristics    C1 C2  ,      , and C3
                     The physical characteristics are Strength, Dexterity, En-
                durance, Grace, Agility, Stamina, and Vigor.
                     All characters have Strength.
                     There are three different characteristics for fine physi-
                cal activity: Dexterity (for humans), Agility, and Grace. Each
                represents a slightly different physical approach.
                     There are three different characteristics for resistance to
                fatigue: Endurance (for humans), Stamina, and Vigor. Each
                represents a slightly different ability.                            ceived load after all modifications. If Burden does not exceed

                                                                                  - 50 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                C6 SOCIAL STANDING OR CHARISMA OR CASTE
                      C6 is the characteristic associated with an individual’s
                place in society.
                      Social Standing (Soc) is social position in large group
                hierarchy. Social Standing indicates social class and the lev-
                el of society from which the character comes.
                      Charisma (Cha) is position in small group hierarchy.
                Charisma reflects a relative social relationship between
                members of a small group. A low Charisma individual will de-
                fer to and follow the leadership of a high Charisma individual.
                      Caste (Cas) is position in genetic group or family hierar-
                chy. In some cases, Caste is established culturally.

                Using C6
                     The C6 characteristic governs social interactions.
                     Bluff. When conducting a plausible bluff (getting past a
                guard; convincing a clerk; establishing credibility) in general
                society, Check Social Standing. If the target of the bluff has
                C6= Charisma, Check Charisma.
                     Living Costs. C6 determines the cost to that individual
                for basic living. C6 times Cr100 equals the typical cost of
                monthly support (food, clothes, lodging, basic entertain-
                ment). But, if C6= Caste, use half-C6. The Benchmarks and
                Money chapters have more information about living costs.
                     Substituting Soc, Cha, and Cas. Social can also be
                used as Charisma at full value, although Charisma uses
                half-Charisma for Social. Social and Charisma are applied
                as Caste=4. Caste is applied as Social=4 or Charisma=4.
                                                                                    (lower case) is initially a Viscount (Viscountess). The next
                Nobility                                                            increase in Soc elevates the individual to E (upper case) and
                     Nobility is the expected noble rank held by an individual      the title increases to Count (Countess; or, Contessa).
                based on Social Standing. Characters with Soc are par-                   Dukes. A character elevated to Soc = f (lower case) is
                ticipants in the widespread social structure of the universe,       a Duke (Duchess). While all Dukes are powerful, some are
                which includes granted or inherited titles of nobility to those     more powerful than others. The next increase in Soc ele-
                with higher values of Soc. Characters with Cha are at the           vates the individual to F (upper case). The title remains the
                fringes of the Nobility system: their equivalent Soc equals         same, but the noble’s power increases.
                Cha / 2. Characters with Cas do not participate in the Nobility
                system; they are culturally or genetically dismissive of such       The Disruptive Effects of Charisma

                Using CS
                     Sanity may be reduced by crisis or trauma. Reductions
                are permanent, but Sanity may be temporarily regained
                through counselling, or with therapeutic drugs.
                     Sanity is Reduced by Crises. When characters en-
                counter Crises (battles, extreme stress, environmental situa-
                tions, psionic attacks, drug interactions, and others), there is
                the chance that Sanity will be reduced.
                     Check Sanity. Failure reduces Sanity -1.
                     Sanity= Zero Is Insanity. When Sanity is reduced to
                Zero, a character is subject to an automatic Mod (= Flux;
                rolled new with every use) for every use of Intelligence and
                C5= Education or C5 = Training (but not C5 = Instinct) and
                the task becomes Uncertain (1D).
                     The Effect of Counselling on Sanity. Sessions with a
                Counsellor (Counsellor skill) may increase Sanity (or rather,
                reinstate Sanity which has previously been reduced).
                     The Effect of Drugs on Sanity. There are a variety of
                drugs with effects on Sanity, including prevention of Sanity


                 Why Different Characteristics?
                    A range of different characteristics allows the creation of sophonts with slightly (or not-so-slightly) different abilities. When
                 a character needs to use a characteristic which he does not have, he can use the analog (that he does have) at half-value
                 (usually; there are some exceptions).
                    For example, a character with Dexterity faces a task that calls for Agility: he can use his Dexterity at half-value (round frac-
                 tions up). For that particular task, some sophont species with Agility is usually better suited to that task; then again, a Human
                 with Dexterity-10 is still better than a sophont with Agility-4.

                                                                                - 56 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                  Characters and Careers
                                 Characters are the central focus of Traveller: they are the alter-egos of the
                              players, and all activity is centered on them.
                     Characters are people: they may be humans, they may be non-human sophonts, or even artificials, robots, or androids.
                They have quantified abilities which define the range of their possible activities. The character is the Traveller personality;
                the player is the person engaged in playing the game. Traveller presents a vast, diverse, but human-dominated interstellar
                universe. This chapter addresses character generation with a focus on human character generation. Character generation
                also applies more-or-less equally to non-humans, including intelligent alien races (sophonts), artificial beings (androids and
                sophontoids), and robots.

                THE PROCESS                                                        characteristics labelled Sanity (San) and Psionics (Psi)
                   Characters are generated through die rolls that:                which are created only as needed.
                                                                                         Characteristic Code. The characteristics are also num-
                     Create Characteristics as personal physical, mental,          bered by their position in the UPP and prefixed by C (for
                and social values that define the individual basic ability to      Characteristic). The first digit in the UPP is C1 (= Strength),
                function.                                                          the second digit is C2, the third is C3.
                     Select Skills, Knowledges, and Talents as abilities                 UPP Universal Personality Profile. The six character-
                which enable a character to deal with the universe. Knowl-         istics are recorded in the six-digit string called the Universal
                edges and Talents are specialized variations of Skills.            Personality Profile; a ready reference for the abilities of the
                     Determine Experience as the history of the charac-            character.
                ter’s life before beginning the game. Traveller creates an               Roll 2D for each characteristic (ignore for the moment
                extensive character prior history detailing a homeworld and        that some non-Humans use other die rolls) and record the
                chronicling education, one or more careers, and perhaps an         result in eHex as a UPP.
                avocation.                                                               For example, player Mitch is creating a new character

                     Record information about a character in detail on the
                Character Card, and in brief summary in the UPP.
                     The Character Card CC is a blank fillform intended as
                                                                                   THE   5   STEPS OF CHARACTER GENERATION
                                                                                           Create a character in the following sequence.
                a ready-reference with extensive information about the char-
                acter. Throughout the game, the information on the CC is               A  . Characteristics. Generate the six personal charac-
                available to the player and the referee.                           teristics. Defer creating the two additional characteristics.
                     The Universal Personality Profile UPP is a shorthand
                description of the character’s characteristics.                        B . Homeworld. Determine the homeworld for the char-
                     Terms. A Term is a standard period of four years. The         acter. For some this is their birthworld; for others this is the
                character generation process is divided into 4-year Terms.         world in which they spent their childhood.
                Traveller uses the standard 4-year Term without regard to

                year lengths.                                                      advanced education or additional training.
                     The Character May Be Non-Human. This text primarily
                applies to Humans and most characters will be Human. On                D  . Career. Select a career and attempt to begin it. If
                the other hand, Character Generation can also be applied to        successful, resolve the career; if unsuccessful, attempt
                non-Human sophonts.                                                a different career. Careers are resolved in terms of four

                A PERSONAL CHARACTERISTICS                                         die rolls) survival, advancement, and retention; the charac-
                                                                                   ter receives some number of skills along the way.

                 them several Knowledges. Education or Training can only              beginning adventures in earnest. A character selects one

                 tainable in Education or Training.                                   characteristics and current experience). Ultimately a deci-

                                       Power Systems   WMD

                                                                                      THE PROCESS
                  Tracked               Sprays              Instrument
                                                                                           Career Resolution is a multi-step process resolved in
                  Wheeled               Unarmed             Other Instrument
                                                                                      Terms of four years each. The process carries the player
                                                                                      through the stages of the career: Beginning, Risk and Re-
                 Gunner               Flyer                Seafarer
                                                                                      ward, Rank, and Continue. At each stage, the player rolls
                  Bay Weapons           Aeronautics         Aquanautics
                                                                                      for success or failure and notes the resulting consequences.
                  Ortillery             Flapper             Grav
                                                                                           The process uses a Target Number against which the
                  Screens               Grav                Boat
                                                                                      player rolls 2D: a result equal or less than the Target is suc-
                  Spines                LTA                 Ship
                                                                                      cessful. Greater than the Target is failure. Some Targets al-
                  Turrets               Rotor               Sub
                                                                                      low Mods (a Mod increases the Target and increases the
                                        Winged
                                                                                      chance of success). If the requirement is met, the Mod may
                cess awards Skill-4 or Knowledge-4. The character begins              be used. For example, Mod +2 if Int 6+. If the character is Int
                Life Stage-3 with the apprenticed Skill or Knowledge.                 6 or greater, the Target is increased +2.

                cific <Service> Operations table provides assignments for
                the Armed Forces.
                      Roll for Assignment four times per Term (for four annual
                assignments). Each assignment provides a Mod. Determine
                the highest value for the Term: applied to Risk and Reward.
                      Medals. The primary rewards in the Armed Forces are
                medals. If the Reward Roll Succeeds, use the unmodified
                Reward roll to determine the Medal; Mod +1 if an Officer.
                      For example, Star Marine Captain Sir Mark Poles
                98998B participated in the Retreat from Jewell. In this Term,
                his highest Mods are Protected Forces Mod 2, and Combat
                2. His Controlling Characteristic is Strength-9. He selects
                Caution Mod+3.
                      He must roll Risk 9 -2 -3 +3 = 7. He rolls 5, is uninjured
                and receives a Campaign Ribbon (The Retreat From Jewell).
                      The signs on the Mods change for Reward, and he must
                roll Reward 9 +2 +3 -3 = 11. He rolls 9 and succeeds. He
                consults the Medals table on line 9+ Officer Mod = line 10
                and receives MCUF Meritorious Conduct Under Fire.
                      The Reserves. A character who leaves a military, naval,
                or marine career is automatically in the Reserves until retire-
                ment at Life Stage 9, at which point he or she receives a
                Reserve Pension.
                      A character in the Reserves maintains his or her last
                held rank as a Reserve Rank (“I’m a Marine Reserve Ser-
                geant.”); There is no process for promotion or advancement
                while in the Reserves.
                      A member of the Reserves is subject to activation for the
                needs of the service.                                               step catalogs them.
                      A character may resign from the Reserves (Check Con-                Personal Weapons. A character who has received
                tinue) and forego its benefits and responsibilities.                Fighter-1 or greater owns one personal weapon, which may
                                                                                    be any weapon appropriate for the skill held. Note the spe-
                AGING                                                               cific weapon.

                Types of Benefits                                                       Life Insurance may be purchased: the premium is MCr1
                     The Career Resolution Process ends with the accumu-          to start a policy and Cr100,000 to update.
                lation of Mustering Out benefits.                                       A Directorship is an appointment to the Board of Direc-
                     Money. Cash represents travel allowances and person-         tors of a large corporation. It is largely ceremonial, requiring
                al savings. Money is shown on a separate column.                  only annual attendance at Board meetings.
                     Passages. Passages represent commercial interstellar               A Directorship provides an annual payment of Cr36,000,
                starship travel allowances provided by a former employer.         high level access to business executives, and deferential
                                                                             - 68 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                treatment at company facilities. Create a name for the Com-
                pany involved: for example: the <worldname> Corporation.
                     Forbidden Knowledge refers to a skill or knowledge
                that is not, should not, or cannot, be mentioned in polite so-
                ciety (polite society defined as groups with average Soc-8 or
                above). Each receipt provides skill-1.
                     For example, a familiarity with the use of machineguns
                (Fighter) is not mentioned in polite conversation.
                     Ship Shares. A ship share represents a fractional own-
                ership in a starship. It may be redeemed upon Mustering
                Out, or it may be retained and redeemed at some later date.
                     A Proxy is a delegation of power (to vote in the Moot,
                the imperial legislative assembly) by one Noble to another.
                The Proxies a Noble holds indicates his Political Power.
                     Nobles uninvolved in the political process transfer their
                voting rights to a political Noble for financial or other consid-
                erations. A Proxy has a value of about Cr100,000 per year.

                Duplicate Benefits
                    A result that duplicates a previous (unwanted or unus-
                able) benefit may be rerolled until a different benefit is re-
                ceived, for example: Wafer Jack, TAS Member, Knighthood.

                ENTITLEMENTS
                     A character may be eligible for specific Entitlements
                (Retirement Pay, Disability Pay, or Pensions).
                     Getting Paid. Any Entitlement may be collected at any
                Travellers’ Aid Society office (at smaller offices, it may take a
                few days). Entitlements are paid in advance: the payment is          sion of Cr10,000 per year.
                made on the first day of retirement and annually thereafter.
                     A retired Spacer or Marine can collect his Entitlement at       Retirement Pay

                Scout Base or Way Station.                                           duty (not the Reserves) for at least 4 terms are eligible for
                     Duplicate Entitlements: A character may receive du-             Retirement Pay based on total combined terms served.

                ary pension, or both Military and Professor’s retirement pay).       activity and begins adventuring.

                                                                                                                                Character Generation


                                                                                                                           Craftsman                 01
                                                                                                                Craftsman: A skilled creator. An artisan. A
                                   CRAFTSMAN                                                               master of a trade, manual art, or the practical ap-
                                                                                                           plication of a science. Master Builder. Craftsper-
                                                                                                           son. Craftsophont. Craftsbeing. Artificer.
                THE CRAFTSMAN’S PASSION
                     The focus of a Craftsman’s activity is creating Master-
                pieces within his craft. He does not roll Risk and Reward.
                     The Controlling Characteristic governs creating the cur-                   CREATING A MASTERPIECE
                rent Masterpiece (and cannot be used again until all of the
                                                                                                              Controlling Characteristics
                others have been used as well).                                                                                                     Must total
                                                                                                              Craftsman Skill
                     Master Points. In each Term, the Craftsman totals avail-                   Master                                              at least
                                                                                                              Up to FIVE Skills at level 6+
                able Master Points (which will be used toward the current                       Points                                              40
                                                                                                               (or Knowledges at level-6)
                Masterpiece). Roll 9D for Master Points or less for success                                                                         Master Points
                                                                                                               (but not languages)
                in creation. If the Craftsman cannot show at least 40 Master
                Points, he cannot attempt a Masterpiece (treat as Failure).
                                                                                                              9D < Master Points

                                                                                                                     Functionary                    13
                                                                                                              Functionary: One who holds an office or po-
                                   FUNCTIONARY                                                          sition of trust or performs a particular function; an

                                                                                                        sor. Administrator.


                FUNCTIONARIES                                                            What Type Of Functionary?
                     The focus of every Functionary is Office Politics and its                The Functionary position is usually associated with a
                associated efforts to preserve and expand the power of a                 prior career: a soldier finds a position in the civilian defense
                specific office.                                                         establishment; a scholar becomes an educational adminis-
                     Each Functionary is charged with supervising or man-                trator; a merchant becomes a functionary at a starport or
                aging the operations of a bureaucracy. The natural conse-                trade administration, an entertainer becomes part of the
                quence of Office Politics is promotion for Success and job               sales and marketing arm of the local entertainment industry,
                loss for Failure.                                                        a scout becomes a civilian at a Scout Base.
                                                                                                                    The Functionary character must
                 A FUNCTIONARY CAREERS                             Functionary Ranks
                                                                       Rank / Title              Auto Skill
