                THE AGING PROCESS
                     Aging affects a character’s physical and mental characteristics, ulti-
                mately reducing them to zero and bringing on inevitable death.
                     Characters are immune to Aging for roughly the first half of their lives.
                                                                                                                     Aging             A
                Once Aging begins, it occurs every four years on the character’s                   Characters naturally grow old and as they do
                birthday.                                                                      they experience the effects of Aging.

                Normal Aging
                     Physical Aging affects all of the Physical Characteristics.
                     Mental Aging affects some of the Mental Characteristics.                         THE STAGES OF LIFE
                     Human Physical Aging affects Strength, Dexterity, and Endurance. It be-          No Life Stage Human Years          Terms
                gins at age 34 (the beginning of Life Stage 5 Peak) and is resolved as an Aging        0 Infant                  0- 1       1/2
                Check.                                                                                 1 Child                   2- 9         2
                     Human Mental Aging affects Intelligence. It begins at age 66 (the beginning       2 Adolescent (Teen) 10-17              2
                of Life Stage 9- Retirement) and is resolved as an Aging Check.                        3 Young Adult*           18-25         2
                     Sophont Physical Aging affects the Physical Characteristics C1 Strength,          4 Adult                  26-33         2
                C2 Dexterity Agility Grace, and C3 Endurance Stamina Vigor. It begins at the           5 Peak                   34-41         2
                beginning of Life Stage 5- Peak and is resolved as an Aging Check.                     6 Mid-Life               42-49         2
                     Sophont Mental Aging affects Intelligence and Instinct (if present). It begins    7 Senior                 50-57         2
                at the start of Life Stage 9- Retirement and is resolved as an Aging Check.            8 Elder                  58-65         2

                  the government.                                       Close Escort 400      3 4     CE-CU44           8 No          116.0
                                                                         Escort Gig    20     0 4
                SHIP SHARES
                     A ship share is a fractional ownership in a starship. Ship shares are acquired as Muster Out benefits, and become avail-
                able immediately, or the eligibility may be saved for some future use. Characters may pool their ship shares (and thus their
                ownership and usage rights); the majority share holder determines the ship type selected. Some acquired ships are provided
                on loan; others are purchased.
                     Loaned Ships. Some ships (per the table) can be acquired on loan, subject to basic eligibility requirements. The Ship
                Shares are expended when the loan is made. The loaned ship cannot be sold or mortgaged and is subject to occasional as-
                signed missions by the organizational owner.
                     Purchased Ships. Virtually any ship (although usually Merchants or Corsairs) can be acquired with Ship Shares: one
                Share acquires 50 tons of the ship (thus, a 200-ton Free Trader requires 4 Ship Shares to acquire full control). Fewer than the
                required shares may be used, with the remainder financed and subject to monthly payments. Several characters with Ship
                Shares may pool their resources to acquire a ship, and they take ownership in proportion to their shares.

                  FAME
                       Fame is the level of recognition or respect society (or specific sub-
                  divisions of society such as the military) holds for an individual. Fame is
                  not a guarantee of instant recognition; it is the likelihood that a search
                                                                                                                      Fame                F
                                                                                                 Determine the fame of characters during the
                  of documents or databanks will return information about the individual       Career process, and after.
                  (and fame may reflect infamy or notoriety).
                       Fame Stacks. A character’s Fame is the sum of all Fame points
                  received to 20; beyond 20, only the highest Fame applies.
                       Expressing Fame. Fame is noted as Fame-<level>. A world fa-
                  mous Entertainer has Fame-10: name recognition anywhere on the MEDALS
                  world on which he performs.                                                     A successful Reward roll in the Armed Forces
                                                                                              provides a Medal in recognition. The table uses the
                  Calculating Fame                                                            unmodified Reward roll from Risk & Reward.
                       Current Fame for an individual is based on a variety of accomplish-        For example, Eneri Dinsha rolls 9 for Reward
                  ments. For example, Rogue with one Failed Scheme (and no other and is successful. He receives MCUF.
                  applicable factors) has Fame = 1 x 3 = 3.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Background Information for Characters
                                  Characters have greater depth than a mere list of numbers and skills. The be-
                              ginnings of this background are provided by the Background tables. Much of the
                              information about characters is digital: discrete values and skill levels.
                     A more abstract and subjective set of information is the character’s background: his origins and motivations; the organiza-
                tions with which he has served, and the small pieces of information or significant objects which have accumulated over time.

                THE BACKGROUND TABLES                                                     Important Events indicate background events which
                     Specific tables are provided for each career to identify       shaped the experience of the character.
                significant details of character background. Each character               Roll 1D once on the appropriate Table and record the
                should consult each table once (and the Educational Institu-        information.
                tions table once for each school attended).                               Former Organizations indicate companies, military or
                     The information is available privately to the player and       naval organizations, or other associations in which a char-
                may be revealed as needed or as appropriate.                        acter has served. Although a character may have served in
                     Referee Discretion. The Referee may create alternate,          many different organizations during the course of a career,
                custom, or edited information and provide it instead.               it is best to concentrate on one which was a profound influ-
                      Variable Data. Some results on the Background tables          ence.
                include the opportunity for variable data: a variety of possible          Roll 1D once on the appropriate Table and record the
                entries. Variable data is identified by <braces>; when pres-        information.
                ent, substitute randomly generated or deliberately selected               Acquired Secrets indicate background or latent infor-
                information. <World> indicates any reasonable World Name;           mation which may ultimately inspire action by a character.
                <Surname> indicates any reasonable personal name.                         A Secret is some object or thing the character finds in
                     Other variable data is similarly generated.                    his possession: it may be something in his files, or taken
                                                                                    from the bottom of a drawer, or a memento. The significance
                Using The Tables                                                    of the object is controlled by the referee in the course of later
                      The five tables include:                                      events.
                      Educational Institutions provide ideas for the names                A Secret is a spark for an adventure. Its description is
                of schools, colleges, and universities. Educational institu-        just enough to direct the adventurers’ attention toward some
                tions give insights into character’s previous activities.           goal. The Secret has some importance; it is generally shared
                      When characters discuss their educations, it is natural       or discussed only with comrades who can be trusted, and
                to compare the schools attended and their relative merit. To        even then only incompletely.
                that end, each school is ranked; higher values are better;                Some secrets are family knowledge, passed down from
                when two schools are compared, their relative rank provides         generation to generation; others are learned in the course
                an indication of their importance and comparative worth.            of adventuring. Roll 1D once on the appropriate Table and
                      For each educational institution attended, roll 1D once       record the information.
                on the appropriate Table (and then roll for the institution’s

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Life Pursuits and Experience
                                  Characters can designate specific areas of interest in their lives and improve
                              them over time.
                    A Life Pursuit is a skill emphasis: a statement that a specific skill is a priority for a specific character. It may be a job,
                hobby, or some other area of importance. A Certificate is a formal statement that a character can succeed at specific tasks.
                    Together, these two concepts allow a character to improve his store of skills through Experience.

                LIFE PURSUITS                                                            How Many Life Pursuits? Life Pursuits are important
                     Life Pursuits express a personal skill emphasis. A Life        interests or abilities. While it is possible to have dozens of
                Pursuit is a brief statement about a specific job or hobby or       Life Pursuits, they would not then express important inter-
                interest, defined by a Title, Primary Skill, Required Charac-       ests. Most characters should have three or four Life Pursuits.
                teristic, C+S, and (optional) Secondary Skills.                          For Reference. A player may ask about a particular Life
                                                                                    Pursuit (“What’s involved in being a Concert Pianist?”). The
                      Life Pursuit                                                  Game Master records a Life Pursuit and marks it “For Ref-
                      Descriptive Title                                             erence.” Players then decide if they want to use that Life
                      Characteristic. Skill. C+S=12.                                Pursuit.
                      Commentary.
                                                                                        Life Pursuit (For Reference)
                     The Title of the Life Pursuit is a job title or occupation         Concert Pianist
                (a few words of description may be included). A Required                Dexterity. Music. C+S=12.
                Characteristic is the one most applicable to the use of the             Primary Instrument = Piano.
                Primary Skill. The Primary Skill is the most appropriate skill,
                knowledge, or talent for the Life Pursuit.                               A Hobby. An otherwise unqualified character may make
                                                                                    one Life Pursuit his hobby even if he does not have the re-
                      Life Pursuit                                                  quired C+S. It is labeled Hobby.
                      Neurosurgeon Doctor in interstellar service.
                      Medical. Dexterity. C+S=12.                                       Life Pursuit (Hobby)
                                                                                        Concert Pianist
                     A character with Medical and Dexterity can have this               Dexterity. Music. C+S=12.
                Life Pursuit if the sum of Medical and Dexterity is at least 12.        Primary Instrument = Piano.

                      Life Pursuit                                                       Shifting Priorities. A Character can shuffle the priori-
                      Competitive Weight Lifter.                                    ties of his Life Pursuits from time to time by consulting with
                      Athlete. Strength. C+S=12.                                    the Referee and indicating the new information.
                                                                                         The Value Of A Life Pursuit. Life Pursuits show the
                     Required C+S. The required C+S for a character’s first         areas that a character cares about in his life. When a Life
                Life Pursuit is 12; enough to always succeed at an Average          Pursuit skill is used, the character is allowed Mod +1.
                task. The required C+S increases +1 in each additional Life
                Pursuit. A character’s third Life Pursuit requires C+S= 12+         CERTIFICATES
                (3-1) = 14. Only the Primary Skill value is used in C+S.                 A Certificate is a formal statement (and an important

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Genetics
                                  The characteristics of Traveller characters have a generated component and
                              an inherited component. The inherited (or genetic) component can be passed from
                              generation to generation by characters.
                    The inherited components (the genes) of the personal characteristics enable a player to create characters in other histori-
                cal milieux with demonstrable links to the player’s primary character. They also allow characters to create children through
                which they can continue role-playing after the demise of the original character. Genetics also allows the creation of clones.

                Important Terms                                                      For humans, Strength is generated with 2D. For some non-
                      The following terms are important for the understanding        humans, Strength may be generated with 1D, 2D, 3D, or
                of genetics.                                                         even 4D.
                      Gene. The individual inherited value for a genetic char-            The first D rolled for a genetic characteristic is the Gene
                acteristic. Human Strength is generated with 2D: the first die       for that characteristic. The remaining D for the characteristic
                (1-6) is the inherited part of Strength and that value is the        represent training, experience, and environment.
                Gene. The second die (1-6) is the developed Strength based                For example, human Strength characteristic is gener-
                on experience and environment. A normal Gene has a value             ated using 2D. 1D is the genetic component inherited from
                from 1-6. Higher and lower values can occur though muta-             generation to generation. The other 1D is the generated
                tion or geneering. A Gene with a value of 0 is defective.            component and rolled on 1D when the character is created.
                      Genetic Characteristic. A characteristic which has             For example, human Eneri Dinsha inherits a Strength gene

                  -5 Litter    Litter    Litter     Litter   Litter   Litter
                                                                                        There is no common ancestor for all life in the universe.
                  -4 Litter    Litter    Litter     Litter   Litter   Litter
                                                                                   Life has evolved independently from the primordial soup on
                  -3 Child     Child     Child      Child    Litter   Litter
                                                                                   many, many worlds. Nevertheless, parallel evolution has
                  -2 Child     Child     Child      Child    Child    Litter
                                                                                   produced life forms which are similar in biochemical and ge-
                  -1 Child     Child     Child      Child    Child    Child
                                                                                   netic structure.
                   0 Child     Child     Child      Child    Child    Child
                                                                                        There are, therefore, many different possible structures
                 +1 Child      Child     Child      Child    Multiple Multiple
                                                                                   for genetic transmission of inherited characteristics. The hu-
                 +2 Child      Child     Child      Multiple Multiple Multiple
                                                                                   man structure is DNA. Other conceivable structures involve
                 +3 Child      Child     Multiple Multiple Multiple Multiple
                                                                                   alternate combinations of amino acids, triple helices, qua-
                 +4 Multiple Multiple Multiple Multiple Multiple Multiple
                                                                                   druple helices, and more.
                 +5 Many       Many      Many       Many     Many     Many

                                   Chimeras
                     Use this checklist to control creation of Natural or Geneered Chimeras. The processes are nearly identical; differences
                (only) for Natural Chimeras are shown. Create a blank Sophont Creation Card for the Chimera being generated.

                Geneered                                                             Natural
                 1. Select Two Sophont Creation Cards                                  1. Select Two Sophont Creation Cards
                    A. with identical Genetic Profile and Nucleic Acid.                   A. with identical Genetic Profile and Nucleic Acid.

                      Combine the Pattern1 PON and Pattern2 PON.                            Combine the Pattern1 PON and Pattern2 PON.

                    Identical Genetic Profile= All Genetic components of the Profile are the same. For example, SDEIES and SDEITC are
                identical because C5 Education and C6 Social Standing are not Genetic and C5 Training and C6 Charisma are not Genetic.
                    Viability. Obvious viability faults include lack of useful senses or lack of useful limbs. Less obvious faults include short life
                spans or abilities not well-adapted to intended conditions.
                    The Technology Of Geneered Chimeras. Geneering is practical at TL 14.

                   Proper identifying information should be added as necessary.

                                                                                                                           Chimeras
                                  A chimera is a hybrid of two or more distinct species, produced naturally or
                              through geneering. Chimeras are distinguishable from clones (duplicates created
                              from existing genetic templates), synthetics (a blend of biological and non-biolog-
                              ical processes), and robots (truly mechanical or non-organic beings).

                    A Chimera (pronounced ky-MEER-ah, for those with                  (not necessarily both sophonts) to create a new organism.

                     is the result of interspecies fertility producing offspring

                                                                                      Playing Chimeras
                                                                                          A Chimera can be played like any other sophont. The
                                                                                      details of its location and origins are created as necessary.

                                                                                      ARE VARGR CHIMERAS?
                                                                                           The Vargr are an intelligent Major Race created by the
                                                                                      Ancients through genetic manipulation of Terran carnivore/
                                                                                      chasers at about the same time humans were scattered from
                                                                                      Terra to the stars. Researchers have confirmed that Vargr
                                                                                      are genetically derived from family Canidae and almost cer-
                                                                                      tainly genus Canis (that is, wolves or proto-dogs).
                                                                                           The Unanswered Question: Did the Ancients so com-
                                                                                      pletely understand molecular biology that they simply wrote
                                                                                      new genes to insert into Earth’s proto-dogs? Or, did those
                                                                                      Ancient genetic engineers do what modern geneers do? Did
                                                                                      they take the nearest available compatible genes for hands,
                                                                                      and upright stance, and increased intelligence?
                 The Vargr of Lair (Kneng-3 [G5 V] A8859B9-F)
                                                                                           Are Vargr Human-Wolf chimeras?
                                                                                - 111 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Synthetics
                                  A synthetic is an organic- or biologically-based artificial being manufactured
                              according to a master template or blueprint. Synthetics blend biological and non-
                              biological processes (the specific proportion may vary). For example, a synthetic
                              may use biological processes to produce energy but have a mechanical pump to
                              circulate blood.
                    Synthetics are distinguishable from clones (duplicates created from existing genetic templates), chimeras (the result of
                genetic engineering), and robots (truly mechanical or non-organic beings).

                Just How Synthetic Is Synthetic?                                    TYPES OF SYNTHETICS

                THE TECHNOLOGY OF SYNTHETICS
                     Effective Cloning and Forced Growth are foundations
                for the organic components of synthetic; these technologies
                cluster around TL-13. Mechanical and electronic compo-
                nents are available at earlier levels.

                                                                                    CREATING SOPHONTOIDS
                 The FOUR Concepts Of Synthetics                                          Sophontoids are created by the factory according to a
                                                                                    model or pattern which details the values for its characteris-
                     To promote synthetics as safe within the community
                                                                                    tics. Typically, sophontoids are created in “batches” of about
                 and responsive to the needs of users, synthetics should
                                                                                    100. Sophontoids from the same batch have a special bond
                 have a series of compulsions that promote proper behavior.
                                                                                    and consider themselves brothers (or sisters or sibs).
                     Synthetics should have a no-harm compulsion.
                                                                                          Creating a sophontoid involves determining what values
                     Synthetics should have an obedience compulsion.
                                                                                    best emulate the pattern.
                     Synthetics should have self-preservation compulsion.
                                                                                          Available Characteristics. The sophontoid manufac-
                     Synthetics should have a work-ethic compulsion.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Clones
                                  A clone is a sophont genetically identical to a single or donor parent sophont.
                              It has the same genes as its parent. Clones fill important social, economic, and
                              medical functions in society. Clones are distinguishable from synthetics (a blend
                              of biological and non-biological processes), chimeras (the result of genetic engi-
                              neering), and robots (truly mechanical or non-organic beings).
                    The concept of clone embodies an organic reproduction or recreation of a single individual. The genes of the original are
                used to create one or more duplicates.

                CLONE TYPES                                                                 Offspring clones are birthed as children and then pro-
                   There are five general types of clones:                            ceed through all normal life stages.
                   Natural. Clones occurring naturally.                                     For example, Merchant Captain Alexander Jamison
                   Offspring. Clones produced as children.                            travelled the starlanes and never settled down; coinciden-

                   Guest. Clones produced to provide cheap labor.                     Life Stage 5, he feels (and acts on) his instincts to have a
                   Med. Clones produced to provide medical parts.                     family. He visits a doctor’s office on Regina and makes the
                                                                                      necessary arrangements. Nine months later, he returns to

                                                                                - 120 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                                   Tasks
                                  Characters face a variety of obstacles and challenges… Characters resolve
                              these many challenges with tasks: Success allows characters to continue their
                              travels and to move on to the next situation; Failure makes characters re-try, or try
                              other tasks instead, or even abandon their current efforts and go in a totally new
                              direction.
                     Tasks dictate the success or failure of the characters’ endeavors. They allow specific activities to be resolved consistently
                whenever, wherever, or however, they occur. It is the responsibility of the players behind the characters to analyze a variety
                of possible tasks and decide on the best course of action.
                     Many Tasks Are Skipped. Many potential tasks can be skipped because their resolution would slow down the game
                without providing any additional drama. The referee determines which situations actually call for resolution and which can be
                assumed to be completed without difficulty or mishap.
                     Skills, Talents, and Knowledges. The terms Talent and Knowledge are special cases or variations of Skill.
                     The Purpose Of The Traveller Task System. The Traveller task system provides a means of resolving situations; the
                details of those situations in light of the tasks, skills, and characteristics become the basis for the story-telling aspects of
                Traveller adventures.

                The Synergy of Skill and Characteristic
                     The Traveller task resolution system considers together the character’s personal aptitudes and individual skills. Char-
                acteristic represents a base of natural ability, Skill represents experience, learning, and practical knowledge. For example,
                different Characteristics interact with a Skill differently: Dexterity and Medical determine success as a surgeon; Education and
                Medical determine success in diagnosis. A high Dexterity and low Education character might still make a good surgeon; a
                low Dexterity and high Education character might make a good diagnostician; a high Dexterity and high Education character
                makes a good well-rounded (and probably more successful) Doctor.

                AN OVERVIEW OF TASKS
                      Tasks are important actions whose results have an effect on the characters and their endeavors.
                      Tasks may be provided in the text of published adventures; they may be stated in the game rules; sometimes they may
                need to be provided by the referee, fragmentarily or definitively. The many forms of expressing tasks are all acceptable.
                      Assets. The Skills, Knowledges, Talents, Characteris-
                tics, and Modifiers used in a task are all Assets. Asset may
                refer to any of these specific terms, or to all of them collec-     MANY TYPES OF TASKS
                                                                                         Cooperative Tasks. Two or more characters perform
                tively.
                                                                                    the task.
                      Creating Tasks. Knowing an activity, the referee deter-
                                                                                         Opposed Tasks. Two or more characters compete to
                mines the appropriate Assets to be considered. The referee
                                                                                    resolve the same task.
                also decides the difficulty of the task which determines the
                                                                                         Tasks Without Skill based on characteristics and one
                number of dice to be rolled.
                                                                                    or more other factors.
                      To succeed, the player then must roll the Target Number

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                 DEFINITIONS OF IMPORTANT TERMS
                 The following terms are used in resolving tasks:
                    Assets. Skills, Characteristics, and Modifiers used in a task are all Assets. Asset may refer to any of these specific terms,
                 or to all of them collectively.
                    Characteristic. The individual character’s Characteristic most closely associated with the skill being used in the task.
                    Die Roll. Die roll is the result of the use of the dice. The number of dice used is dependent on the Difficulty.
                    Difficulty. The expression of how hard the task will be to complete.
                    Knowledge. A body of information based on a field of science or experience. A Knowledge is a variant form of Skill.
                    Mods. Modifiers. Modifiers are additions or subtractions reflecting local conditions. Modifiers may include weather, envi-
                 ronment, distractions, and other elements.
                    Skill. The individual character’s Skill being used to accomplish the task. The term Skill often includes Knowledges and
                 Talents.
                    Talent. A personal ability not generally possible for a Human, but possible for some non-humans. A Talent is a variant form
                 of Skill. Talent is not used in the sense of fine art ability (such as a talent for music).
                    Target Number. The Target Number is the sum of all Assets used in the Task. The player must roll the Target Number (or
                 less) on the Dice in order to succeed.
                    Task Cycle. The process of evaluating which tasks to undertake.

                                                                                              Absolute duration (as on a timed test) passes whether
                                                                                         the task succeeds or fails.
                                                                                              Variable Duration. Some durations cannot be accu-
                                                                                         rately forecast. The task may have a duration of minutes,
                                                                                         hours, or even days, or more.
                                                                                              The standard times for variable duration are 10 min-
                                                                                         utes, an hour, a day, and a week.
                                                                                              Rarely is a single task duration more than 10 hours; if a
                                                                                         longer time seems appropriate, break the task into compo-
                                                                                         nents. These durations can be multiplied: for Two Weeks,
                                                                                         resolve 1 week twice = 2 to 22 days.
                                                                                              Variable duration is resolved even if the task failed.
                                                                                              For example,

                                                                                             To Replace A Flat Tire (Variable 10 minutes)
                                                                                             Average (2D) < Strength + Driver

                                                                                             The task may take as little as (10 -5 = ) 5 minutes or as
                                                                                         long as (10 + 5 = ) 15 minutes, but it averages 10 minutes.

                SPECIAL APPROACHES TO TASKS                                         SPECTACULAR RESULTS
                    Tasks may require special preparation.                            Beyond The Normal Description. An element of chance
                    Training, Practice, and Rehearsal. Preparation for              which goes beyond the normal description of a task. For
                a task through rehearsal (usually used with Performance),           example, a spectacular result to a task to repair a piece of
                practice (used with Athletics), training (from an expert), or       equipment reflects that very small chance that the task will
                studying (cramming before a test or exercise) can be used           go very right (and something else is corrected as well) or
                as a die modifier. The modifier depends of the situation, but       very wrong (and a vital part is dropped down a drain).
                such modifiers range from 1 to 3.                                     Beyond The Normal Task. The spectacular result reach-
                                                                                    es beyond the narrowly focused task itself. For example,
                      To cram for a test                                            when an automobile accident avoidance task succeeds, the
                      Difficult (3D) < (Characteristic + Skill)                     accident is avoided; a spectacular success talks of narrow
                      Study materials required.                                     escapes, brilliant maneuvering, and heart-pounding effects
                      Success allows Good Flux Mod on Test                          on the participants.
                                                                                      Independent of Skill. Even the most skilled of charac-
                    To practice for a task                                          ters can experience spectacular failure and even the least
                    (the same requirements as the true task)                        skilled of characters can experience spectacular success.
                    Success produces Good Flux. If multiple practices are
                attempted, only the best Mod is used.                              Spectacular Failure
                                                                                         A task result may be terribly bad. Spectacular Failure
                      Tools and Equipment. Many tasks cannot be per-               not only fails to achieve the goal of the task, but produces
                formed without tools or equipment. Tasks may specify spe-          some negative consequences (an injury to the characteristic
                cific tools or types of equipment required.                        being used; a cost in money, or to reputation; or damage
                                                                                   to the tools or equipment being used). Spectacular Failure
                      To surgically remove an appendix                             requires some long-term detrimental consequences to the
                      Hopeless (6D) < (Dex + Medical)                              Characteristic or the Skill used in the associated task.
                      Only household instruments available.                              Three Sixes. If the actual dice roll includes 3 sixes (not
                                                                                   possible on 1D or 2D), the result is a Spectacular Failure.
                      To surgically remove an appendix                             The task fails to produce the results desired, and it produces

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                 WHEN TASKS FAIL
                   Ultimately, a task determines if a character can succeed in activities that they believe are necessary to their plans. When
                 tasks fail, or fail repeatedly, the characters (and the players behind them) must search in role-playing fashion for tasks at
                 which they can succeed.
                   Consequences. For any but the most ordinary of tasks, failure carries with it potential consequences.
                   The most obvious consequence is that the activity cannot be completed, or the fruits of the task are not achieved.
                   Task failure has consequences in addition to the simple lack of success. The individual may receive an injury or wound.
                   If the Task is Dangerous, the task roller may receive a greater injury.
                   If the Task is Destructive, the equipment involved may be damaged or destroyed.
                 MISHAP                                DANGEROUS                                        DESTRUCTIVE
                 When a Task fails, roll               When a Dangerous Task fails, roll                When a Destructive Task fails, roll

                       Flux + Reliability                          Flux + Safety                                    Flux + Safety
                   If negative, the user suffers a       If negative, the user suffers a Severity=         If negative, the user suffers a Severity=
                 Severity= Easy Injury accompa-        Flux + Safety roll. If greater than Difficulty   Flux + Safety roll. If greater than Difficulty
                 nied by Diagnosis= 1D.                of the original task, convert to 0= no effect.   of the original task, convert to 0= no effect.
                   Use Reliability for a device          Use Safety for a device used in the task;         Use Safety for a device used in the task;
                 used in the task; otherwise R=0.      otherwise S=0.                                   otherwise S=0.

                 INJURY
                      Injury reduces personal Characteristics (usually the Characteristic used in the Task first). If injury reduces one Charac-
                 teristic to zero, apply remaining Injury to other Characteristics.
                      Injury may be non-physical: it may affect the Mental or even the Social Characteristics.
                      If total Injury reduces only one Characteristic to more than zero, the Injury heals naturally in about a week.

                 ACTIONS
                 Task-like activities which do not depend on skills or characteristics are Actions. An action is expressed like a task, but the
                 Task Statement is more free form. It indicates the information which will be compared against the difficulty level.

                 THE SENSE ACTION                                           THE AGING ACTION
                    Sense actions (Vision, Hearing, Smell) evaluate           Characters age as time passes. Aging is consulted at the begin-
                 circumstances to determine if a specific sense, in a       ning of each Term once aging begins.
                 specific situation, succeeds in seeing or hearing (or        Aging is resolved with the Aging Action.
                 otherwise) some object.
                    The Senses are resolved as Actions. Two types of           To Age
                 Action are possible: At Range, and In Contact.                Average (2D) < Life Stage
                    At Range. When senses operate at a distance
                 (Vision, Hearing, Awareness, Perception), the Action          Success inflicts the effects of age on the character.
                 takes account of Range by using D6 equal to the               A character wants to FAIL this action.
                 range number (Vshort=1, Short =2, etc and a range
                 table is provided).                                          For example, at Life Stage 5, a character rolls for each of the
                    In Contact. When senses operate in contact              physical characteristics: If the result is 5 or less on 2D, the charac-
                 (Touch, Smell), range is ignored and the Action is         teristic is reduced by -1 (in this case, failure to age is a benefit for
                 based on 2D.                                               the character).
                    Sense Actions are detailed under The Senses.              Aging is detailed within Character Generation.

                 Tasks and Skills


                 MS                 Master
                                    Skill List
                                                                          A skill is a statement of ability based on a job, vocation, or interest.
                                                                        A knowledge is a body of information based on a field of science or
                                                                        experience. A talent is a personal ability not generally possible for a
                                                                        human, but which may be possible for some specific non-humans.
                  Benchmarks. One level of a skill or knowledge or talent represents about one year of experience, education, or training. A
                character with Skill-4 has four years of experience in that skill; Knowledge-3 is the equivalent of three years of instruction or
                practical experience with that field of knowledge.

                                                                             - 132 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                          Skills Define Abilities
                                 Skills are quantifications of each individual character’s abilities. Skill is a broad
                              label which is further divided into Skills, Knowledges, and Talents. Each has its
                              own usages and restrictions.
                      Skills are areas of expertise. A character who has a skill is capable of acting within that area. For example, someone
                with Medic can reasonably be expected to attempt (and sometimes or often succeed) in medical situations. In the general
                role-playing sense, characters often do things that do not involve tasks but do involve skills. For example, a starship owner
                will probably not hire an astrogator who does not have Astrogator. There is no task involved, but having the specific skill is
                nevertheless important.
                      Skills are Assets in the Resolution of Tasks. A character with a specific skill can attempt tasks that someone without
                skill is not even permitted to try.

                In This Chapter                                                          Default skills represent this base of activity that even an
                     The skills that characters may learn are covered in this       untrained person may be willing to attempt.
                chapter alphabetically. Each entry shows the skill name. If              A skill identified as a Default skill may be used by any
                the skill is a cluster or cascade skill, that notation is made.     character. The skill level used is 0 (zero); other penalties also
                     The Master Skill List. The Master Skill List shows the         apply when resolving a task using a Default skill. But, the
                available skills.                                                   chance of success is still better than if the task were not at-
                     Task Examples. Because skills are intimately bound up          tempted at all.
                in tasks, many of the entries in this chapter include examples           Default skills are sometimes called Level-0 Skills.
                of tasks using the skills. These examples are not exhaustive.            The standard Default Skills are automatically available

                     Education Knowledges. In the Education system, it is
                not possible to acquire Animals, Driver, Engineer, Fighter,
                Flyer, Gunner, Heavy Weapons, Language, Pilot, or Seafarer
                directly; Education provides only the contained Knowledges.
                     The Sciences. Some characters have the opportunity
                to learn a specific Science through the Education process.
                Sciences are limited to a maximum level of 6.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                The Skills
                      Skills are the primary means by which characters do things in Traveller. Each character has a variety of skills, and the
                higher a skill rating, the more expert the character is with that skill. With training, any character can eventually become profi-
                cient at any skill.
                      The Skill descriptions here provide insights into their application and their use with tasks. The descriptions are not exhaus-
                tive, and the Referee has considerable flexibility in applying them to game situations.

                ACTOR                                                               ment). An Actor takes on a part, which may be within a stage,
                     Actor (Act, Acting, Drama) is expertise in the dramatic        voice, or video production, or may be a component of a live
                arts, including an ability to adopt a personality or role, to       action event.
                convince an audience of a viewpoint, and to convey a wide                Acting Tasks. Acting tasks benefit from rehearsal.
                range of emotion while involved in the role or part.                Many acting tasks are hasty and few are cautious.
                     Characters with Actor have learned to conceal their true            Related Skills. Actor is one of the six Arts (Actor, Artist,
                selves, their motives, and their emotions while manipulat-          Author, Chef, Dancer, and Musician).
                ing an audience into believing whatever persona or emotion
                they are portraying.                                                ADMIN

                one of the Trades is a skilled worker (as opposed to an un-

                BROKER
                    Broker is skill in the marketing of goods, and represents
                an understanding of the business of buying and selling.
                    Characters with Broker act as agents for the owner of
                goods (and may act for themselves); when the sale takes
                place, the broker receives a commission.

                      To find a buyer for goods.
                      Difficult (3D) < Int + Broker

                Trade and Commerce
                     Broker is used in the Trade and Commerce process.
                     Broker is about negotiating the best deal between a
                buyer and a seller. In its simplest form, the Broker transac-
                tion entails very little work while earning a commission. The

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                       Tasks and Skills
                                                                                                                Gravitics
                LANGUAGE
                     Language is skill in communications between sophonts
                using a commonly accepted symbol set.
                                                                                                         Skills Gunner
                                                                                                                Heavy Wpns
                                                                                                                                                    1
                     Native Language is (usually) Anglic. Every charac-                                         Language
                ter has a native language: the one learned in childhood and
                probably the one currently used. The level of skill in a native
                language is equal to the higher of the character’s Intelligence
                or Edu (not C5).
                     By default, a character’s native language in Traveller is Anglic. Anglic is a form of English evolved over the course of
                thousands of years and heavily influenced by other languages it has encountered. If a character does not specifically choose
                another language, his or her native language is the default language.
                     Human Eneri Dinsha 777777 has Anglic as his native language. He speaks and understands Anglic-7.
                     Additional Languages (other than through Education). When Language is received (other than in Education), the in-
                dividual selects a specific foreign language other than his or her native language. The first receipt of Language is at one level
                less than the character’s Native Language. Each additional receipt of Language allows selection of another language at one
                level less than the previous. Language skill level received = Native minus number of Language receipts. For example, a char-
                acter 777777 has English as his native Language. He receives Language five times, and takes each in a separate language:
                He has English-7, Spanish-6, French-5, German-4, Italian-3, Swedish-2.
                     If the character elects, additional receipts of Language can instead increase a non-native language, but never to more
                than Native Language.
                     For example, Eneri Dinsha’s native language is Anglic; his skill is Language (Anglic)-7. He can speak it well and has a
                reasonable vocabulary. He occasionally makes simple grammatical errors. During one of his careers, he receives Language.
                He selects Vilani (the other major language in the Imperium) and receives it at one level less than his Native Language. He has
                Language (Vilani)-6. He receives Language a second time: he uses it to increase Vilani (to Language (Vilani)-7). He receives
                Language a third time. It cannot be used to increase Vilani beyond his Native Language, so he selects Gvegh and receives
                (level= Native minus number of receipts = 7-3= 4) Language (Gvegh)-4.
                     Reading and Writing. Language includes the ability to read and write, and includes an ability to input through appropriate
                keypads, touchpads, and other input devices.
                     Technological Devices may have an ability to input, process, and output in various languages. Where necessary, a de-
                vice will include a statement of its Language capability. By default, most devices are dual-labelled in Anglic-9 and Vilani-9 and
                can (with minimal difficulty) process these two languages.
                     Non-Human Languages. Some languages are impossible for Humans: they operate with senses Humans do not have,
                or beyond the range that that Humans can experience. It is possible for any character to learn a language even if it cannot be
                expressed or sensed; there are technological devices which can make up for physical inabilities.
                     Recording Languages A Character Knows: Record this skill as Language (Specific)-Skill. For example, Language
                (Spanish)-4 or Language (Gonk)-3. Space constraints may force Vilani-4, or Gonk-4 where the meaning is clear.
                     Related Skills. Language and Linguistics are related: Language is the ability to hear, speak, and use a specific language.
                Linguistics is the specialized study of all languages.
                 TYPICAL LANGUAGES
                 Language Users               Description
                 !kee      K’kree             The trade language spoken by most space-faring K’kree.
                 Anglic    Imperial           One of two standard Imperial languages: derived originally from the English.
                 Battle    Imperial           The spoken and gesture battlefield language of the Imperial Star Marines.
                 Flash     Kursae             The Perception-based Poice language of the Kursae.
                 Gonk      Geonee             The spoken language of the Geonee, a Human minor race heavily involved in imperial trade.
                 Gvegh     Vargr              The most commonly encountered Vargr language, spoken by most Vargr.
                 Mariel    Plexxan            An Awareness-based language; typically written or printed in Mag-based inks.
                 Oynprith  Droyne             Ancient ritual language of the Droyne.
                 Sagamaal Sword Worlds        The most common language of the Sword Worlds, derived from Icelandic.
                 Tezapet   Darrian            The Darrian spoken language.
                 Trokh     Aslan              The Aslan spoken language.
                 Vilani    Imperial           One of two standard Imperial languages: derived from the spoken language of Vland.
                 Zdetl     Zhodani            The Zhodani spoken language.

                      Language as a Major or Minor in Education. When a specific Language is specified in Education as a Major or Minor,
                it is acquired at double rate and is not restricted by the character’s Native language level.
                      Education may be used to increase native language level.

                MUSICIAN
                      Musician (Music) is ability in the auditory arts.
                      Musician is a skill in the creation of entertaining sounds,
                including the ability to play a musical instrument, to sing (or
                hum, or whistle), and to convey a wide range of emotion
                while playing music.

                BIOLOGICS                                                                                    Biologics
                      Biologics (Biologic) is concerned with devices based on
                living matter. Example biologic devices include grown hull
                panels, interior shock absorbers, water purifiers, carbon di-
                oxide scrubbers, and motion sensors. It includes activities

                     A knowledge is a body of information based on a field of science, training, or experience. For example, Chemistry reflects

                The Talents
                    A talent is a personal ability not generally possible for a human, but which may be possible for some specific non-humans.
                The maximum level for a specific talent is usually 15

                Going Into Hibernation
                     Hibernation may be voluntary or involuntary.                      Failure in each case results in feeble or ineffective at-
                     Voluntary. Hibernation may be a voluntary act. The in-       tempts by the subject (rather than outright refusal).
                dividual makes a conscious decision to hibernate. He finds a           Post Hypnotic Suggestions. Hypno may be used to
                safe place (his bunk; a remote corner somewhere) and falls        implant reasonable post hypnotic suggestions which remain
                asleep with some plan for total sleep time (in weeks).            in effect on the subject for (subject’s) C3 days.
                     Involuntary. Any wounding which would otherwise kill              Mass Hypnosis. A Hypno user may (attempt to) affect
                the character converts to involuntary hibernation. The in-        multiple subjects simultaneously, with difficulty increasing
                dividual enters hibernation automatically. Hibernation lasts      based on Group Size.
                weeks equal to C3. Upon emergence, characteristics are
                returned to normal.                                               INTUITION

                 QREBS

                                      Equipment                           THE QREBS EQUIPMENT EVALUATION SYSTEM

                 Q1                   Evaluation
                                                                              Individual items of equipment (“objects”) can vary widely due to
                                                                          the differences between manufacturers, and the different emphases
                                                                          they give to design and quality.
                                                                              The QREBS (pronounced “krebs”) system evaluates items for five
                essential characteristics: Quality, Reliability, Ease Of Use, Bulk (or Burden) and Safety.
                      Objects. An item subject to QREBS is called an object: it may be a device, a piece of equipment, a machine, an item,
                some sort of apparatus, or just a thing. While QREBS is primarily concerned with devices and machinery, it can also apply to
                (judiciously omitting some parts of the system) plants and animals, artwork, even books, drama, or music.
                      Multi-Component Objects. Where several objects are combined into a larger item (for example, the components of a
                groundcar), the proper use of the QREBS system is to treat each major subsystem separately (motor, steering, brakes, com-
                fort systems, and others).


                 Q QUALITY (AND PERIOD)                                                        Q  Quality is a measure of the workmanship of an
                  2D-2          Description         Mod    Period                        object. Reflects the Period between reliability downgrades.
                                Garbage      - 6 or less   Non-functional                     Quality= 2D-2. Or as specified otherwise.
                      0         Very bad              -5   Minutes                            Quality Mod= Q minus 5. (= Quality converted to Flux
                      1         Bad                   -4   Hours                         for use as a Mod).
                      2         Poor                  -3   Days                               When an Object fails, reduce its Quality by -1.
                      3         Lesser                -2   Weeks
                      4         Below average         -1   Months                              P   Period is the time between Reliability downgrades.
                      5         Average                0   Six Months                         As each Period ends, reduce object Reliability by -1.
                      6         Better than some +1        One Year                           For example, an Average quality object has a period
                      7         Better than many +2        Two Years                     of 6 Months; it is reduced in Reliability -1 every six months.
                      8         Very good             +3   Three Years                        When an Object fails, reduce its Period -1 (= more likely
                      9         Better than most      +4   Four Years                    to fail again).
                     10       A Excellent             +5   Ten Years                          Period may be suspended when the object is in storage
                     11       B Superb                +6   Twenty Years                  or non-use under circumstances which do not promote wear
                     12       C Masterpiece           +7   Centuries                     or degradation.


                 R RELIABILITY                       E EASE OF USE                       B BULK / BURDEN                 S SAFETY
                      Description                          Description                        Description                    Description
                  - 5 Very unreliable                  - 5 Very difficult to use          - 5 Very easy-to-carry          -5 Very hazardous
                  - 4 More unreliable                  - 4 More difficult to use          - 4 Easier to carry             -4 More hazardous
                  - 3 Unreliable                       - 3 Hard to use                    - 3 Easy to carry/wear          -3 Hazardous
                  - 2 Somewhat unreliable              - 2 Somewhat hard to use           - 2 Better than many            -2 Somewhat hazardous
                  - 1 Slightly unreliable              - 1 Slightly difficult to use      - 1 Better than some            -1 Slightly hazardous
                    0 Reliability neutral                0 Ease of use neutral              0 Burden neutral               0 Safety neutral
                  +1 Better than some                  +1 Better than some                +1 Slightly unergonomic         +1 Better than some
                  +2 Better than many                  +2 Better than many                +2 Unwieldy                     +2 Better than many
                  +3 Reliable                          +3 Easy to use                     +3 Hard to carry                +3 Safe to use
                  +4 More reliable                     +4 Easier to use                   +4 More burdensome              +4 Safer to use
                  +5 Very reliable                     +5 Very easy to use                +5 Very burdensome              +5 Very safe
                    Reliability is the de-               Ease of Use is the facil-          Burden is the apparent         Safety is the inherent
                 pendability of an object.           ity (Mod) for operation of          or felt weight of an object.    danger presented by an

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                                   QREBS
                                 Objects vary widely in quality and usefulness. This variation gives seemingly
                              identical objects a variety of positive and negative attributes. The QREBS system
                              details these differences.
                    Despite the uniformity which mass production techniques impart to their output, individual pieces of equipment can vary
                widely due to the differences between manufacturers, and the different emphases that they give to design and quality.

                THE QREBS EQUIPMENT EVALUATION SYSTEM
                   The QREBS (pronounced “krebs”) system evaluates pieces of equipment for five essential characteristics:

                                           Q                   R                 E                  B                   S
                                          Quality          Reliability     Ease-Of-Use            Burden              Safety
                                           QUA                REL              EOU                  BUR                SAF
                                          =2D -2             =Flux            =Flux                =Flux              =Flux
                                          0 to 10           -5 to +5         -5 to +5             -5 to +5           -5 to +5


                     Objects. Any item which is subject to QREBS is called an object (or a device, a piece of equipment, a machine, an item,
                or an apparatus). QREBS is primarily concerned with devices and machinery, but it can also be used (judiciously omitting
                some parts of the system) with plants and animals, artwork, even books, drama, or music.
                     Multi-Component Objects. Where several objects are combined into a larger item (for example, components combined
                to become a groundcar), the proper use of the QREBS system is to treat each major subsystem separately.

                THE QREBS FORMATS                                                    THE FIVE QREBS VALUES
                    Present values for QREBS in standard format so refer-                 QREBS values (except for Quality) are Flux values from
                ees, players, and users can quickly understand them                  - 5 to +5. Quality is a number (2D-2) from 0 to 10. Low or
                                                                                     negative values are poor or bad; high or positive values are
                Full QREBS                                                           good (Burden is an exception; low Burden is better).

                      QREBS = 4 -4 -4 +4 0                                                Quality is an overall measure of workmanship.
                      Write the individual values (preceded by + / -).                    Reliability is compared to Flux for mishaps.
                      Notice that the first digit Q Quality has no sign.                  Ease of Use is a Mod on tasks using an object.
                                                                                          Burden is a Mod on the felt weight of an object.
                Individual QREBS Values                                                   Safety is compared to Flux for Dangerous and Destruc-
                     qreBs =        -4       Capitalize the appropriate value.       tive Mishaps.
                     Burden =       -4       Write out the value as a word.
                     BUR =          -4       Use the abbreviation.                   Q QUALITY
                     B=             -4       Write the value as a letter.                 Quality is a measure of the consistency of workman-
                     Use Equal to avoid confusing a minus with a hyphen.             ship, merit, value, or worth of an object. It directly reflects the
                     (B = - 4 rather than B - 4; B = +1 rather than B +4).           time period between reliability downgrades.
                     The essential rule for QREBS values is: state the value              Determining Quality. Quality may be pre-specified for
                in an unambiguous way to ensure easy comprehension.                  an object; if not, it is generated with 2D-2.
                                                                                          Quality Mod. The Quality Mod (= Q minus 5) is a Flux
                                                                                     equivalent to Quality and a Mod when using the object.

                      Reliability measures the dependability of an object.            Why Is It Hard To Use?

                 AN OBJECT IN USE
                                                 Q            R              E             B            S
                                               Quality      Reliability Ease-Of-Use      Burden       Safety
                 Assumed                         5              0             0              0           0
                 Inspected                     Q+Flux        R+Flux        E+Flux        B+Flux       S+Flux
                 In Use (after one day)         Q±1         Assumed         E±1           B±1        Assumed
                 In Use (after first mishap)                  R±1                                      S±1
                    Assumed: With no other information, a user assumes the values shown.
                    Inspected: Inspection can reveal elements of QREBS for an object.
                    In Use: Any user can understand at least some of the QREBS values for an object through the process of using it.
                    Quality is easily identified. A user knows this value within one point after a day of use.
                    Reliability is not easily determined. Assume a value of zero. If inspected, assume that value. After its first experienced
                 mishap, reveal the true value within one point.
                    Ease Of Use is easily identified. User knows this value within one point after about a day.
                    Burden is easily identified. A user knows this value within one point after a day of use.
                    Safety is not easily determined. Assume a value of zero. If inspected, assume that value. After its first experienced mis-
                 hap, reveal the true value within one point.
                    ± (Within One Point): Roll Flux: if Zero, then the true value is known. If a positive value, reveal the true value plus 1; if a
                 negative value, reveal the true value minus 1.



                Why Is It Easier To Carry?                                           EVALUATING OBJECTS
                     Objects with a low Burden are easier to carry.                      The QREBS values for an object are initially unknown
                     Handles. An attached handle facilitates carry.                  (and assumed to be QREBS = 5 0 0 0 0).
                     Straps. A strap or sling allows the object to be carried
                in a slung position without using the hands or manipulators.         Inspecting An Object

                jects to be carried without using the hands or manipulators.         an hour to carefully look it over, test its operation, disas-
                     Ergonomic. Some objects are naturally (or deliberate-           semble and reassemble it looking for flaws or problems. The
                ly) configured so that they are easy to carry or manipulate.         inspector must have a skill related to the object.

                S SAFETY                                                             of the QREBS values. For each QREBS value,
                     Safety measures an object’s inherent safety or danger.
                     Safety expresses the possibility, even probability, that            Roll Flux plus the QREBS value.
                the use of an object will inflict pain, injury, or even death.
                                                                                         An as-inspected QREBS for the object is then created,
                      Safety = Flux. Or as specified otherwise.                      randomly substituting one correct value for each level of in-
                                                                                     spector skill (to a maximum of 4). The referee (through the
                     For example, a Respirator Safety= - 5 lacks a battery           inspector) reveals this final value.
                reserve and has no low energy warning; its compressor can
                catastrophically fail and send metal shavings into the user’s        Company or Corporate Reputation

                stroys the object.                                                   dicate the QREBS values for devices (which can then be

                                   Using                                  IMPLEMENTING QREBS

                 Q2                QREBS
                                                                                Any object can be evaluated for QREBS using the simple proce-
                                                                          dure provided here.
                                                                                MOARN. There is no need to determine QREBS for every ob-
                                                                          ject. Evaluate objects only as really necessary.
                CALCULATING QREBS

                         Q                 R                E                 B                  S                    Wa                    P
                        Quality         Reliability     Ease-Of-Use         Burden              Safety              Working Age            Period
                         QUA              REL              EOU               BUR                 SAF
                        =2D -2           =Flux             = Flux           = Flux              =Flux               = Good Flux          = Quality
                                    adjust R= R-Wa
                   1.    Quality = 2D-2. Usually ranges from 10 Excellent to 0 Very Bad.
                   2.    Reliability = Flux. For a New object. Ranges from +5 Very Reliable to - 5 Very Unreliable.
                   3.    Ease Of Use = Flux. Ranges from +5 Very easy to use to -5 Very difficult to use.
                   4.    Bulk/Burden = Flux. Ranges from +5 very burdensome to -5. Very easy to carry
                   5.    Safety = Flux. Ranges from +5 Very safe to -5 very hazardous.
                   6.    Period = Quality. Determine the units of time from the Quality Table.
                   7.    Working Age= Good Flux (in Periods).
                   8.    True Age = from Table.
                   9.    Adjust Reliability (= subtract Working Age; drop fractions).
                 TRUE AGE                               Experi-        AGING WITH QREBS
                 Flux Current Antique Artifact Surplus mental
                  -5     50     400      RR       100      20
